perturbation.original_text: No later than thirty (30) days following the Closing, each of AWP and the Company shall change its respective corporate name and trade name and cause its organizational documents to be amended to remove any reference to "Armstrong."
perturbation.changed_text: Each of AWP and the Company will have the option to change its respective corporate name and trade name and cause its organizational documents to be amended to remove any reference to "Armstrong." There is no set time frame.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'no later than thirty (30) days' deadline creates uncertainty and could violate state laws requiring timely updates to corporate filings.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires that certain documents, including amendments to certificates of incorporation, be filed promptly. Removing the 30-day deadline contradicts the general requirement for timely corporate filings, potentially leading to non-compliance with Delaware law. Also, not changing the corporate name or trade name could imply a continued affiliation with Armstrong, which is misleading.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the mandatory 30-day deadline for changing the corporate name and trade name, making the action optional and removing any time constraint. The law explanation suggests that this contradicts Delaware law requiring timely filings, and could imply a continued affiliation with Armstrong, which is misleading. While the provided snippet focuses on the formation and content of the certificate of incorporation under Delaware law ("§ 101. Incorporators; how corporation formed; purposes."), it sets the stage for mandatory compliance, indirectly supporting the claim that removing a deadline for corporate changes creates a contradiction. The changed text introduces uncertainty about when these changes must be made.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall within the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within forty-five (45) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that Arizona declines to institute any Proceedings against third-party infringers or violators of any Arizona Licensed Patents, regarding activities that would fall within the Company Field if conducted by the Company, within ten (10) days after being notified or becoming aware of such infringing conduct, the Company or Buyer shall have the right to institute any Proceedings against such third-party infringers or violators.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the period from 45 to 10 days might not provide Arizona with adequate time to assess the infringement and decide whether to pursue legal action, potentially conflicting with their right to manage and control their intellectual property.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 35 U.S. Code § 286 - Time limitation on damages
perturbation.law_explanation: This section dictates the time frame for patent infringement damages. By shortening Arizona's decision period to 10 days, the company/buyer's right to sue could potentially cut into Arizona's ability to gather adequate data. Ten days may not be sufficient time for Arizona to assess the infringement, gather evidence, and decide on a course of action. This limited time might preclude them from seeking full damages for past infringement if the company moves forward with legal action prematurely.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 35 U.S. Code § 286 - Time limitation on damages | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 286 - Time limitation on damages U.S. Code Notes prev | next Except as otherwise provided by law, no recovery shall be had for any infringement committed more than six years prior to the filing of the complaint or counterclaim for infringement in the action. In the case of claims against the United States Government for use of a patented invention, the period before bringing suit, up to six years, between the date of receipt of a written claim for compensation by the department or agency of the Government having authority to settle such claim, and the date of mailing by the Government of a notice to the claimant that his claim has been denied shall not be counted as part of the period referred to in the preceding paragraph. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 813 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on Title 35, U.S.C., 1946 ed., § 70, part (R.S. 4921, amended (1) Mar. 3, 1897, ch. 391, § 6, 29 Stat. 694 , (2) Feb. 18, 1922, ch. 58, § 8, 42 Stat. 392 , (3) Aug. 1, 1946, ch. 726, § 1, 60 Stat. 778 ). The first paragraph is the same as the provision in R.S. 4921 with minor changes in language, with the added provision relating to the date for counterclaims for infringement. The second paragraph is new and relates to extending the period of limitations with respect to suits in the Court of Claims in certain instances when administrative consideration is pending. perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the time Arizona has to institute proceedings against infringers from 45 days to 10 days. The Perturbation Explanation states this might not provide Arizona with adequate time to assess the infringement and decide whether to pursue legal action, potentially conflicting with their right to manage and control their intellectual property. The LLM Law Explanation expands on this, saying 10 days may not be sufficient time for Arizona to assess the infringement, gather evidence, and decide on a course of action. This limited time might preclude them from seeking full damages for past infringement if the company moves forward with legal action prematurely. While the provided law snippets (35 USC 286) focus on the 'Time limitation on damages' and specify a six-year limit for infringement recovery, they do not directly address the timeframe for assessing infringement. However, because the modified text potentially interferes with Arizona's ability to gather adequate data, assess infringement, and decide on a course of action, the change creates an inconsistency that undermines Arizona's control over its intellectual property rights, creating an inconsistency contradiction as defined by the perturbation type.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within thirty (30) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to remedy such breach within ten (10) days' written notice thereof; provided, however, that if the material breach of this Agreement by the breaching Party is limited to the Licensed Copyrights, Licensed Know-How, the Arizona Licensed Patents, or the Licensed Trademarks, the non-breaching Party shall be entitled to termination solely with respect to the affected part of the license (i.e., in such case, the non-breaching Party may terminate this Agreement with respect to the Licensed Copyrights or the Licensed Know-How or the Arizona Licensed Patents or the Licensed Trademarks, as applicable).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period to 10 days could be considered insufficient time to remedy a material breach, especially for complex issues, potentially conflicting with general contract law principles of fairness and reasonable opportunity to cure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code § 2-608. Revocation of Acceptance in Whole or in Part.
perturbation.law_explanation: While this agreement is about intellectual property rather than goods, UCC 2-608 provides a useful parallel. It dictates that a buyer can revoke acceptance of goods if the non-conformity substantially impairs the value of the goods AND they accepted it without discovery of such non-conformity AND their acceptance was reasonably induced either by the difficulty of discovery before acceptance or by the seller's assurances. It also stipulates such revocation must occur within a reasonable time after the buyer discovers or should have discovered the ground for it. While not directly applicable, the reduction to ten days from 30 days to remedy a breach related to IP infringes upon the 'reasonable time' period. Depending on the nature of the breach and the steps needed to cure it, 10 days is simply not a reasonable time period. The agreement is governed by Delaware law as stated in 13.5.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. perturbation.justification: The original text allows 30 days to remedy a breach, whereas the changed text reduces this to 10 days. The Law Explanation states that this timeframe potentially conflicts with providing a 'reasonable time' to cure a breach, particularly for complex IP issues. Although no specific law snippet explicitly defines 'reasonable time' in this context, the very nature of the 'reasonable time' standard is undermined by an arbitrary reduction to 10 days, indicating the creation of an inconsistency that could lead to legal disputes and unenforceability.
